question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with gas set to exactly GAS_SCALE_FACTOR (10,000), causing scaleGas() to return 0 (since gas <= GAS_SCALE_FACTOR returns 0L), and then have gasLimitBucket.tryConsume(0) called which in bucket4j always returns true without consuming any tokens, completely bypassing the gas throttle for that transaction?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the RequestProperties counter (AtomicLong counter) which increments on every test() call regardless of filter match, by sending requests that match a filter with a very high limit (Long.MAX_VALUE default), causing the counter to eventually overflow to negative values after 2^63 requests, making counter.getAndIncrement() >= limit evaluate to false and permanently disabling the limit check?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with the gas field to submit transactions with gas values that, after scaleGas() division, produce token counts that exactly match the remaining gasLimitBucket capacity, causing a precise exhaustion attack that leaves the bucket at exactly 0 tokens and blocks all subsequent legitimate transactions for the remainder of the refill window?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that rateLimitBucket uses no SynchronizationStrategy (unlike gasLimitBucket which uses SYNCHRONIZED), causing concurrent requests to race on rateLimitBucket.tryConsume(1) and potentially allow more than requestsPerSecond transactions to pass the rate check before the synchronized gasLimitBucket check, creating an inconsistency between the two throttle layers?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a transaction with a gas value that causes scaleGas() to return a negative number due to integer overflow (e.g., if gas is Long.MIN_VALUE), causing gasLimitBucket.tryConsume(negative) to throw an exception or behave unexpectedly, potentially crashing the request handler or bypassing the throttle check entirely?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with the data field of a ContractCallRequest to include a very long hex string (up to the maximum allowed by @Hex validation) that matches a RequestFilter with FilterType.CONTAINS, causing the Strings.CI.contains() case-insensitive comparison to perform O(n*m) string matching on large inputs, leading to CPU exhaustion and request processing delays?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the two-phase throttle check (rateLimitBucket first, then gasLimitBucket) by sending requests that pass rateLimitBucket but fail gasLimitBucket, causing the rateLimitBucket token to be consumed without a corresponding gas token consumption, effectively wasting the rate limit budget of other users without performing any actual EVM computation?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: opcodeRateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with the transactionIdOrHash path parameter of the opcodes endpoint to send requests with valid-looking but non-existent transaction hashes, consuming the opcodeRateLimitBucket token (1 RPS) before the service discovers the transaction doesn't exist, effectively blocking all legitimate opcode trace requests for that second?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the RequestProperties.test() method's random sampling (RandomUtils.secure().randomLong(0L, 100L) >= rate) for non-THROTTLE actions to statistically bypass LOG or REJECT filters by sending enough requests that the random check eventually passes, allowing the attacker to submit transactions that should be rejected at a rate of (100-rate)% of attempts?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the restore() mechanism by crafting transactions that trigger the 'result == null' branch in restoreGasToBucket(), causing the full gasLimitToRestoreBaseline (100% of gasLimit by default) to be restored to gasLimitBucket, and then immediately submitting new transactions before the next refill cycle, effectively getting free gas budget recycling?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with the value field of a ContractCallRequest to a non-zero amount while omitting the from field, exploiting the hasFrom() validation logic to cause a validation exception that is processed before throttle checks, allowing the attacker to probe server behavior without consuming rateLimitBucket tokens?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that gasLimitBucket capacity equals gasPerSecond (after scaling), and by submitting a single transaction with gas set to exactly gasPerSecond * GAS_SCALE_FACTOR, consume the entire gas budget in one request, blocking all other users from submitting any transactions for the entire refill window?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of authentication on the from field in ContractCallRequest to impersonate any Ethereum address, bypassing RequestFilter rules that use FilterField.FROM with FilterType.EQUALS to block specific addresses, and submitting transactions as if they originated from a trusted or whitelisted address?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the scaleGas() floor division to submit transactions where gas is set to (N * GAS_SCALE_FACTOR - 1) for large N, causing the scaled token count to be N-1 instead of N, effectively getting one extra unit of gas per GAS_SCALE_FACTOR boundary crossed, accumulating significant unaccounted gas over many requests?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: opcodeRateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the opcodeRateLimitBucket's refillGreedy behavior to send exactly 1 opcode request per second in a tight loop, consuming the entire 1 RPS budget continuously and preventing any other user from ever successfully calling the opcodes endpoint?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with the block field of a ContractCallRequest to use a far-future or invalid block number, causing the EVM execution to fail after the throttle check has already consumed tokens from rateLimitBucket and gasLimitBucket, wasting the throttle budget of other users without performing useful work?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the concurrent restore() calls in a multi-threaded environment where multiple failing transactions simultaneously call gasLimitBucket.addTokens(), and since addTokens() on a SYNCHRONIZED bucket may not cap at the original capacity, cause the bucket to accumulate tokens beyond its configured gasPerSecond limit, granting subsequent requests more gas budget than intended?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a ContractCallRequest with a data field that is a valid hex string but contains a function selector matching a known expensive precompile, bypassing any RequestFilter that only checks for specific byte patterns, and submitting it within the rateLimitBucket allowance to cause disproportionate backend resource consumption?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the two-bucket architecture (rateLimitBucket + gasLimitBucket) by sending many low-gas transactions (gas=21000, scaled to 2 tokens) that each consume 1 rateLimitBucket token and 2 gasLimitBucket tokens, exhausting the gasLimitBucket (750,000,000 scaled tokens) before the rateLimitBucket (500 tokens) is exhausted, causing a disproportionate denial of service on the gas dimension?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with the to field of a ContractCallRequest to an empty string (allowEmpty=true per @Hex annotation), bypassing RequestFilter rules that use FilterField.TO with FilterType.EQUALS on specific contract addresses, and submitting contract deployment transactions that would otherwise be filtered?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that gasLimitBucket is initialized at application startup with a fixed capacity from throttleProperties.getGasPerSecond(), and by sending a burst of maximum-gas transactions immediately after server startup before the bucket has been partially consumed, monopolize the entire gas budget for the first second and deny service to all other users during the initial burst window?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the RequestProperties.test() counter (AtomicLong) which is shared across all requests and never resets, by sending requests that increment the counter past the configured limit (Long.MAX_VALUE default), causing the filter to permanently stop matching after the limit is reached, effectively disabling REJECT or THROTTLE actions for that filter rule?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user tamper with submitted transactions by setting gas to the maximum allowed by evmProperties.maxGasLimit (15,000,000), causing scaleGas() to return 1,500 tokens per request, and submitting 500 such requests per second (within rateLimitBucket), consuming 750,000 gas tokens per second against a gasPerSecond budget of 750,000,000 scaled tokens, revealing that the gas throttle is effectively unconstrained for the default rate limit?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: opcodeRateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the opcodeRateLimitBucket by sending requests with valid gzip Accept-Encoding headers but malformed transactionIdOrHash values that cause expensive database lookups before failing, consuming the 1 RPS opcode budget while causing disproportionate database load per request?
